title: 15th FC v 16th FC
lead: Similar in ==vertical devolution (41%)== but sharply different in ==horizontal formula, grant design==, and the philosophy of ==‘equity vs performance’==.

items: Both give states ==41% of the divisible pool== — the 16th FC imparts a ==‘semi-permanence’== to the figure.
Divisible pool = gross central taxes ==minus collection costs, cesses and surcharges== — the 16th FC grapples more explicitly with the ==shrinking effective pool==.
==‘Grand bargain’ (16th FC)==: states accept a smaller percentage in return for a ==larger pool== if the Centre merges cesses/surcharges into shareable taxes.
16th FC ==discontinues revenue deficit, sector-specific and state-specific grants== — replaced by ==rule-based, performance-linked instruments==.

head: Criterion
15th FC
16th FC
Implication
rows: Slight dilution of pure equity; still the dominant redistributive factor.
More weight to population-linked expenditure needs.
16th FC drops the explicit effort criterion.

blocks.text: Net effect: a ==rebalancing of equity and efficiency== — equity retained through income distance, efficiency added through the GDP-contribution criterion.
